prompt:
Let $a, b,$ and $n$ be positive integers with both $a$ and $b$ greater than or equal to $2$ and less than or equal to $2n$. Define an $a \times b$ cell loop in a $2n \times 2n$ grid of cells to be the $2a + 2b - 4$ cells that surround an $(a - 2) \times (b - 2)$ (possibly empty) rectangle of cells in the grid. For example, the following diagram shows a way to partition a $6 \times 6$ grid of cells into $4$ cell loops.

| P   P P   P | Y   Y |
| P | R R | P | Y | Y |
| P | R R | P | Y | Y |
| P   P P   P | Y | Y |
| G   G G   G | Y | Y |
| G   G G   G | Y   Y |

Find the number of ways to partition a $10 \times 10$ grid of cells into $5$ cell loops so that every cell of the grid belongs to exactly one cell loop.

Positive real numbers \( x, y, \) and \( z \) satisfy the following equations:

\[
xyz = 3,
\]
\[
(x-y)(y-z)(z-x) = 4,
\]
\[
(x+y)(y+z)(z+x) = 40.
\]

Compute the minimum possible value for \( x \).

Give the minimum value of x as an exact expression using only integers, rational numbers, and radicals (no trigonometric or inverse trigonometric functions, and no approximations).

An oriented graph is a directed graph with no pair of opposite arcs. For a digraph $D$, let $fvs(D)$ be the minimum size of a vertex set whose deletion makes $D$ acyclic, and let $\Delta(D)$ be the maximum (total) degree, i.e., for each vertex $v$, $\deg(v)=\deg^+(v)+\deg^-(v)$ and $\Delta(D)=\max_v \deg(v)$. Define
\[
c^*:=\inf\Bigl\{c\in\mathbb R:\ \forall\text{ oriented graphs }D\text{ on }n\text{ vertices with }\Delta(D)\le 4,\ fvs(D)\le c\,n\Bigr\}.
\]
What is the exact value of $c^*$?

A subset $X$ of $\mathbb{Z}$ is called rich if for any positive integer $n$ and $n$ numbers $x_0, x_1, \dots, x_n$ belonging to $X$, all integer roots of $x_0 + x_1 \cdot x + \dots + x_n \cdot x^n = 0$ belong to $X$. Find all rich sets that contain $2^k - 2^l$ for any positive integers $k$ and $l$.

Determine the least positive integer \(m\) for which one can choose \(m\) pairwise distinct positive integers \(a_1,\ldots,a_m\) such that
\[
\prod_{i=1}^{m}\left(1-\frac{1}{a_i}\right)=\frac{42}{2010}.
\]
Determine the quotient obtained when \(x^9 + 1\) is divided by \(x - 1\).

A row of 100 empty boxes, labeled \(B_{1}, \ldots, B_{100}\), is placed on a table, and there is an unlimited supply of pebbles. For a positive integer \(n\), Alice and Bob play this game.

During the first round, Alice takes \(n\) pebbles and places them among the 100 boxes in any way she chooses. Every later round has the following two stages:

(a) Bob selects an integer \(k\) satisfying \(1 \leqslant k \leqslant 99\), thereby dividing the boxes into \(B_{1}, \ldots, B_{k}\) and \(B_{k+1}, \ldots, B_{100}\).

(b) Alice chooses one of the two resulting groups, puts one additional pebble into each box in that group, and takes one pebble out of each box in the other group.

Bob is declared the winner if, after any round ends, at least one box has no pebbles in it. Determine the least \(n\) for which Alice has a strategy that prevents Bob from winning.

In 3-dimensional coordinate space, consider the points on the plane \(x+y+z=75\) whose coordinates obey
\[
x-yz<y-zx<z-xy.
\]
This set is made up of three disjoint convex regions, and precisely one of them has finite area. If that finite area is \(a\sqrt{b}\), where \(a\) and \(b\) are positive integers and \(b\) is not divisible by the square of any prime, determine \(a+b\).
Suppose y satisfies 3y + 2(1 + y) = 17. Determine the value of 6y + 5.
